title: Paradigm C + Paradigm D = The Stock Market Is Going To Bubble

If you think about the six cycles that matter to financial markets, it's growth, inflation, policy, our monetary policy, fiscal policy, liquidity, and positioning. Growth, you shape the economy, that means it's going to be better a year from now. Uh inflation, uh the market is um the the deflation is a problem uh in terms of the delta, but highly likely to peak within a year, and most importantly, the Fed on a monetary policy framework standpoint is amending its reaction function to inflation in a way that makes the inflation risk the acceleration of inflation, uh it mitigates that risk. So, and we know monetary policy is getting incrementally supportive. We know fiscal policy will get incrementally supportive starting early next year. So, that's essentially three of those four cycles are explicitly positive. One is so positive that it's overwhelming the negativity from the inflation cycle in terms of monetary policy. Um so, obviously, that all augurs well for liquidity. When those four cycles are doing on a net basis doing good things for the economy and asset markets, liquidity is going to go up. Like liquidity is basically the result of how those four cycles interplay with each other. And so, we know liquidity is moving in the right direction on a one-year forward basis. And so, the only thing left that can really top of the markets is the positioning cycle, which is admittedly uh as full as it was back in in February, uh you know, before the crash. Uh but again, we don't have there's no there's no real threat. Like, what's the You In order for positioning and valuation are not catalysts. They are accelerants when cat when there is a catalyst. And my And the point I'm making is that the five cycles that matter that would ultimately cause a significant reversal in the momentum and dispersion of asset classes from a positioning cycle standpoint, they're all moving in the right direction. So, you To me, I think about where the market could be a year from now. Like, why wouldn't the market bubble? We should wind up in a bubble. It should It should bubble. Like, why aren't we going like we're at 20-something times earnings in S&P? Like, why aren't we going to 30? We should go to 30. This is secular transition to AI and in a huge productivity boom. That I mean, obviously, it's not going to be great for society at the end of it, but like again, this is labor versus capital, capital is winning. The stock market is not a market of labor, it's a market of capital. And so, that's my my general take on this. It's like, when you see this kind of activity in the in in in the internals of the market, which arguably the best certainly the best trader of all time and one of the best investors of all time, Stanley Druckenmiller, told us that this is the best economists in the world. The best economists in the world, the internals of the stock market, are telling you that something is getting very good and at accelerating rate. You know, so this gets the ratio, you know, broke out to a new high uh as well, corrected, and and and bottomed at the previous high. That is as textbook bullish as you get. I'm not even a technical analyst. I don't know anything about technical analysis. But, I'll tell you right now, actually I do know something about technical analysis, I know that this is bullish. We use our global liquidity models to nowcast the leading indicators of private and public sector liquidity across geographies. So, uh just highlight one quick thing here, uh liquidity is currently trending higher globally in Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Eurozone, India, not in Japan, Switzerland, UK, and US. So, that's a lot of liquidity trending higher. So, I wouldn't want to fight that as a bear. Um and then, key leading indicators of global liquidity currently signal significant uptrend over the medium term. So, also be aware of that. For example, in that note we you know, already know where we highlighted uh the potential for the market to bubble, we saw that, you know, you're starting to see some real speculative excesses. That was the That was the signal that '99 was about to happen. So, I would highly recommend uh not shorting anything. You don't have to believe in KISS or Dr. Mo. Uh you know, you don't have to listen to Darius Dale guide your help you guide your portfolio to to the greener pastures. But, if I were you I would definitely listen to the high beta low beta ratio cuz if we broke out through through those that level, that to me would be a very very high risk-on signal uh in the context of all the positive dynamics we continue to observe uh from all the key macro cycles that matter that are represented here in this global liquidity monitor. One of my core you know, fundamental things that I rely on as an investor is where will we be a year from now? And what's the probability of the market crashing in between now and the year from now? And there you know, based on the position cycle, that probability is reasonably high. But, it's also a reasonably high probability that based on our research that we'll be way higher. So, think about that. There's a It's like a very wide distribution. You know, you have a very high probability of from a research perspective that the markets risk assets should be much higher in price. But, you also have historically crowded bullish position that says between now and a year from now the market can crash easily. And that's [snorts] that's hard to deal with. I'm not going to lie. That's very difficult to deal with. So, it puts extra emphasis on dispassionately executing signals like KISS and Dr. Mo because as much as Darius Dale the research analyst might be bullish, you know, incrementally bullish relative to what we already had been since we authored the, you know, kickstarted the 42 macro paradigm C Bull Market back in April. You know, I don't think anybody's been more bullish on Global Wall Street than I have since April or actually May 3rd is just technically speaking uh since May 3rd. Uh I'm more bullish today than I was on May 3rd. But, that doesn't mean the market has to go straight up. The market can go sideways or down. You know, Darius doesn't have to be right on his fundamental views. You know, our fundamental views are incredibly well researched, but that doesn't mean I'm going to be right. It's just a game of probabilities and and constantly accessing and reassessing the probabilities and how the distribution of probable outcomes is evolving. And based on my near two decades of collaborating with some of the world's best institutional investors and you know, all the tools that I have in my tool kit and the drawers that I have in my brain where I'm opening and closing and you know, putting storing information, I'm telling you, paradigm C and paradigm D are as about as bullish as anything you're ever going to see in your lifetime. Doesn't mean it have to go straight up markets, but it's as about as bullish as you're ever going to see in your lifetime. So, uh just be even if you're bearish, you're worried about valuation, you know, just be open to the possibility that the markets could bubble over the next year. Also be open to the possibility that the markets could bubble next year and crash along the way, much like they did in 1998. Don't forget, we had a crash in 1998, market rallied vicious V bottom off that crash and went up 100% 1999. You could easily see something like that. So, uh I don't know. I don't know what the path the market's going to take from here, but I'll tell you right now, it's about as bullish as anything you're ever going to see in your lifetime. The paradigm C and paradigm D being merged together, so >> including myself. I should have brought my bubble. >> [laughter] >> my bubble blower. No, look, I mean, I'm being tongue in cheek about it, but what it does represent is it represents an erosion of central bank independence. Now, they're going to couch it as reserve management operations or whatever they want to couch it as, but the reality is is the federal government's balance sheet and the growth of the federal government's balance sheet has grown has gotten too large, and so ultimately we need the Fed to take pressure off the system in order to, you know, help sustain the bull markets and assets, to sustain growth in the in the real economy. And ultimately this again, like I said, this is a very significant change in US monetary policy. From our perspective and the perspective of many of the top institutional investors on global Wall Street, there's really only six things that impact asset markets. Growth, inflation, monetary policy, fiscal policy, liquidity, and positioning. Growth is already improving. Inflation is going down. It's a tailwind currently. We just saw the amount of change in monetary policy. That's good. Fiscal policy has been tightening, but it's going to be easing significantly next year. The liquidity cycle is already still in the in the uptrend, and the Fed is adding more liquidity to the system. So, the only one of the five cycles that's an issue right now is the positioning cycle. And we see historic degree of crowded bullish positioning, but we know the positioning cycle is not a catalyst. So, when you have all that good news in all the other key macro cycles that matters, it suggests that the risk is that the markets can start to bubble. >> Interesting. So, you're saying in your view that bubble, which a lot of people would have an instinct to maybe lean away from or short, uh, but in your view it sounds like it's just going to expand from here. >> When bubbles are inflating, you want to be long them. >> [laughter] >> When bubbles are inflating, go buy them. It's pretty straightforward.
